Q1: I tried to keep a fairly consistent routine, honestly. I'd usually set aside time in the mornings and some evenings to go through lecture recordings and materials. With five online courses running simultaneously, I had to be somewhat organized or I'd drown. I'd block out about 26 hours a week for studying, though I'll admit my anxiety sometimes made me spend that time less productively than I wanted.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but it definitely shifted when deadlines approached. I'd try to stick to my schedule, but sometimes the anxiety would kick in and I'd either study way more intensely or find myself paralyzed and unable to focus at all. When multiple assignments piled up across my courses, the regularity went out the window and I'd just scramble to keep up.
Q3: Usually it was checking if new materials had been posted or if there were announcements about upcoming deadlines. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't missed anything important—my anxiety makes me paranoid about that. The gamified task notifications actually caught my attention more than I expected, which surprised me because I don't usually go for that kind of thing.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I logged in when I needed to, maybe a couple times a week, but it never became automatic for me. I'm more of an auditory learner, so I'd download the lecture recordings and listen to them elsewhere. The LMS felt more like a checkpoint than a daily habit.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me. I'd listen to them multiple times, sometimes while commuting or doing other things. Being able to hear the professor explain concepts really clicked with my learning style. The discussion forums were occasionally useful too, though I didn't post much—I mostly read what others wrote to see if I was understanding things correctly.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials that were just PDFs of textbook chapters. I have good resources at home, so I'd often use my own textbooks instead. The supplementary videos that weren't lecture recordings I also skipped because they felt redundant. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably didn't prioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. The gamified tasks actually surprised me—I thought they'd feel gimmicky, but they broke things down in a way that reduced my anxiety about the material. If something felt like busywork, I'd mentally check out.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. My anxiety would make me worry that maybe they weren't really optional, or that I'd miss something crucial. But with low motivation and five courses, I had to make choices. Required work got done; optional stuff I'd look at briefly and then move on unless it seemed immediately useful.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost all the lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone constantly. I'd also use my own textbooks and sometimes look up YouTube explanations for topics that weren't clicking. A lot of my studying happened with headphones in, walking around or lying on my bed—none of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing material for hours but the LMS would show nothing. I'd download a lecture once, then replay it five or six times over the week. Or I'd be working through problems in my physical textbook while referencing notes I'd written during those audio lectures. The LMS only saw that single download.
Q11: All the time. That was basically my standard approach. I'd grab the audio files or any downloadable materials in one session, then work with them offline for days. My actual engagement with the content was way higher than my login frequency would suggest. It's just more comfortable for me to study without being tethered to the platform.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel overwhelmed even though I knew I had the time and resources to handle it. Sometimes I'd just freeze up and waste hours worrying instead of actually studying. Those weeks my attendance and engagement definitely dropped.
Q13: The workload across five online courses was genuinely challenging, even with good study habits. When I felt fatigued or confused about something, I'd sometimes just close the LMS and step away rather than push through unproductively. My anxiety made confusion feel worse than it probably was, so I'd need breaks to calm down before I could actually learn anything.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd be watching a lecture video but my mind would be racing with worry about other assignments or just general anxiety. I'd get to the end and realize I'd absorbed maybe half of it. The time showed up in my watch minutes, but the actual learning wasn't there until I went back and listened again later.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly, even though I knew participation mattered. Something about putting my thoughts out there publicly triggered my anxiety. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more confident about my understanding, and then later never came. Assignments I couldn't avoid, but anything with flexibility got pushed back.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, my low motivation meant I'd drift. But as deadlines approached, my anxiety would kick in and suddenly I'd be very focused—sometimes too focused, staying up late and stressing myself out. The LMS would probably show spikes of activity right before due dates, which is pretty accurate to how I operated.
Q17: Feedback did matter to me, though I sometimes dreaded checking it because of my anxiety. When I got positive feedback, it actually boosted my mood significantly and made me more willing to engage with the next assignment. Negative or critical feedback would send me into a spiral for a bit before I could productively use it. The gamified task feedback was surprisingly easier to handle—less personal somehow.
Q18: The structure helped and hurt in different ways. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework, which I needed. But the sheer volume of content across five courses meant I had to be strategic about what I actually engaged with deeply versus what I just completed to check a box. More flexibility might have helped, but also might have made my procrastination worse.
Q19: The lecture recordings being downloadable was huge for me—that made engagement so much easier given how I learn. What made it harder was when materials were only available to view online, or when participation required real-time interaction. My anxiety doesn't do well with synchronous pressure. The gamified elements actually helped break down intimidating tasks into manageable pieces.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and keeps checking things without actually submitting, or someone who's confused and searching through materials trying to understand but not finding what they need. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels, looking busy but not actually moving forward.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My login frequency was pretty low, but I was still engaging with the material extensively offline. Someone looking at my LMS data might think I was barely participating, but I was listening to lectures repeatedly and working through problems on my own. The system just couldn't see that work.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, whether you complete things on time. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually thinking or just going through motions. They definitely miss offline studying, emotional state, and whether the learning is actually sticking. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious or invested in figuring something out, not just anxious about grades. For me, real engagement often happens when I'm listening to a lecture for the third time and something finally clicks. It's internal—the LMS can't measure that feeling.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything—the hours I spent worrying instead of working, or how a gamified task actually helped calm me down enough to learn. It wouldn't show my offline studying, which was probably the majority of my actual learning time. And it definitely wouldn't capture how surprised and relieved I felt when I realized I was going to pass despite everything.
